channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: Why the Future of Video Games is Moving Back to the Dinner Table
gist: Two-part TWiST episode: Bren Putnam (CEO, Board) details a $399 face-to-face touchscreen gaming console that recognizes electronics-free physical game pieces and is monetized via a game store, IP licensing, and a creator subscription; AJ Piplica (co-founder/chairman, Hermeus) lays out the capital-efficient, iterative path to an unmanned hypersonic aircraft (Darkhorse) funded by a $350M Series C and customer-paid flight-test revenue. Despite the title's board-game framing, half the substance is defense/hypersonics.

themes.theme: Board console tech moat and unit economics

insights.claim: Board's $399 console recognizes unlimited physical game pieces that contain no electronics or sensors, using a proprietary conductive pattern of capacitive and non-capacitive plastic plus an in-house embedded-AI vision stack to overcome touchscreens' standard 10-touch-point limit.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: The electronics-free piece + custom CV model is the defensible moat investors should probe; it lowers per-piece BOM cost and enables a high-margin collectibles/accessories business on top of low-margin hardware.
insights.quote: They have a unique conductive pattern built into the surface that uses a special blend of capacitive and non-capacitive plastic, um, and is a unique manufacturing uh process that we've developed in-house.

insights.claim: Board sells the $399 hardware at low-to-no margin and builds LTV through $35 games (each with custom piece sets), accessories like cases and piece storage, premium pieces that unlock in-game content, and a creator subscription launching end of 2026.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: This is a razor/razor-blade console model; the investable question is attach rate and recurring revenue per device, not hardware margin.
insights.quote: selling the the product at low to no margins, and looking to the games, the accessories... as a way to really build long-term, um, LTV off of the customer.

insights.claim: Putnam reports 85% of Board members are active monthly, playing over 30 sessions per month, framing the value against a $100-150 family movie outing.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: 30+ monthly sessions at 85% activation is a strong early engagement signal for a 7-month-old hardware product; if durable it underwrites the content/subscription LTV thesis.
insights.quote: 85% of our members are active monthly paying playing over 30 sessions per month.

insights.claim: Board sold out its 10,000-unit D2C launch run within a few weeks over last holiday season and sells exclusively through its own site board.fun rather than via Kickstarter or retail.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Owning the D2C channel preserves margin and customer data; a fast sellout validates demand but 10k units is still small-scale, so the scaling/supply question is open.
insights.quote: we sold out of our our 10,000 launch units within a few weeks of our launch

insights.claim: Board reached launch in about 2 years by partnering closely with a tier-one contract manufacturer in Thailand experienced with display products (clients cited: Peloton, Portal, Nook), rather than owning the full supply chain as Putnam did at her prior company Mirror.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Leveraging a CM's existing display expertise compressed the hardware timeline; a useful playbook signal for hardware-startup diligence on time-to-market.
insights.quote: we work with a a tier one CM out of Thailand um who had a lot of experience with display products um working with folks like Peloton or Portal or Nook.

insights.claim: Putnam's prior company Mirror manufactured in Mexico rather than Asia because the product's weight and size made trans-Pacific shipping costs erode the savings, and the Mexican factory (a women-run digital-signage maker) delivered high quality.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Near-shored high-quality tech manufacturing in Mexico offers tariff and supply-chain resilience for bulky hardware; relevant to reshoring theses amid China geopolitical risk.
insights.quote: with Mirror it was such a large and heavy object that sort of the shipment from Asia really cut into sort of the benefits of of producing overseas. So we produced in Mexico.

insights.claim: Putnam advises hardware founders not to over-engineer early products but instead deliver the core experience through 'hacky' non-functional prototypes to validate the experience and raise capital.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: A capital-efficiency heuristic for hardware diligence: founders who validate experience before full functionality de-risk the run-out-of-money failure mode.
insights.quote: I generally recommend that you don't over-engineer your early startups. You actually try to show and deliver the experience that you're trying to give to your customers and then build into that once you have more capital.

themes.theme: Board content, IP licensing and platform strategy

insights.claim: Board describes itself as 'Nintendo meets Roblox meets Lego meets a great collectibles business,' with a creator product (SDK usable with AI coding tools) letting users design custom pieces and games, monetized as a subscription add-on.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Stacking collectibles + UGC creation on a hardware base mirrors how Apple Services re-rated Apple; the multi-business model is both the upside and the execution risk.
insights.quote: We're sort of like Nintendo meets Roblox meets Lego meets a great collectibles business.

insights.claim: Board's community game marketplace launches its first title next month with mixed monetization (rev-share in some cases, outright game buyout in others) gated by a quality/safety review rather than a fully open platform.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: The app-store economics are still TBD; investors should watch whether a curated rev-share marketplace can scale supply without diluting quality.
insights.quote: in some cases it's a rev share, in some cases we're buying the game outright.

insights.claim: Board is in conversations with major IP holders and is pursuing a 'Monopoly Go' style reimagining of beloved characters native to its piece-based format rather than straight ports, with releases planned over the next few years.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Premium IP licensing (Star Wars/LOTR-class) is the potential mass-market catalyst; the multi-year timeline tempers near-term revenue expectations.
insights.quote: we are really excited about sort of like Monopoly Go strategy where you take IP and characters and worlds that are really beloved and you reimagine them for board in a way that feels native and true to the technology

insights.claim: Board is launching a larger D&D tabletop product in July 2026, with Putnam signaling that positive reception could open a 'much larger market' via bigger surface formats.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Hardware form-factor expansion (tables, premium large units) is the platform-scaling vector; July D&D launch is a near-term datapoint to watch.
insights.quote: we launched a D&D product a table a tabletop product comes out in July which obviously really lends itself to a larger, you know, a larger surface, a larger map.

insights.claim: Board positions itself as new-category creation aimed at full-family entertainment rather than serving existing board-game or hardcore-gamer communities, explicitly accepting that purists reject it.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Category-creation plays (Calacanis cites Airbnb, Uber, eBay with near-zero initial TAM) are hard for TAM-bound VCs to price; the bet is on inducing a new market, not winning an existing one.
insights.quote: This is new category creation, so we take the interactivity of video games and we take the tactile feel of playing with physical pieces

insights.claim: Mirror, Putnam's prior smart-mirror fitness company, was sold to Lululemon for roughly half a billion dollars about a week before COVID hit.
insights.speaker: Bren Putnam, CEO of Board
insights.so_what: Establishes founder credibility and a prior winning exit; the pre-COVID timing underscores how connected-fitness valuations were path-dependent.
insights.quote: we actually sold about a week before COVID.

insights.claim: Calacanis argues the core VC failure mode is inability to back products that don't fit an existing TAM, citing that Airbnb, ride-sharing and eBay each had near-zero initial addressable markets and induced demand into existence.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis, host
insights.so_what: A reusable diligence lens: discount TAM objections for genuine category-creation bets and weight customer delight signals instead.
insights.quote: you have to ignore people who are trying to create a TAM for something that doesn't exist in the world. The TAM for Airbnb was basically zero. They induced a market to exist.

insights.claim: Hermeus is building Darkhorse, an unmanned reusable hypersonic aircraft faster and smaller than the SR-71 designed to operate with near-impunity in contested airspace against adversaries like China, keeping pilots out of harm's way.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Frames Hermeus as a defense-asymmetry play (stealth-era successor); the uncrewed angle de-risks the political cost of pilot loss/POW capture that justified the SR-71.
insights.quote: Darkhorse is a hypersonic reusable aircraft uh faster than the SR-71, smaller than the SR-71 with no person on board.

insights.claim: Hermeus's Chimera engine is a turbine-based combined-cycle system that pushes an off-the-shelf F-100 gas turbine 30-50% past its design speed (to ~Mach 3) using an in-house pre-cooler that drops ~800F intake air, then transitions to a ramjet (shockwave compression, no mechanical compressor) for higher speeds.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: The turbine-to-ramjet mode transition is the central technical moat; only Lockheed/Aerojet Rocketdyne have done it (in lab) and the SR-71's J58 only partially, so success would be a genuine first-in-the-sky.
insights.quote: a ramjet is literally the simplest air propulsion device you can think of... you're using shockwaves, which naturally occur when you're flying faster than the speed of sound. Those do the compression for you.

insights.claim: Piplica frames Darkhorse as effectively a reusable first stage for munitions, noting the Mark II's own range is similar to existing fighters but releasing weapons like the AMRAAM from high speed and high altitude roughly doubles or more than doubles their range.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Reframes the value proposition from aircraft speed to weapon-range extension ('range is the name of the game'); the real defense ROI is multiplying existing munition reach.
insights.quote: existing weapons double-ish the range, maybe more.

insights.claim: Piplica identifies long-duration high-speed flight (sustaining Mach 3-5 while managing continuous heat buildup in a closed loop and reusing the airframe over many cycles) as the single hardest and last major problem before a production Darkhorse.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Pinpoints the key remaining technical risk for diligence: thermal management is a flipped version of reentry (lower peak temp, far higher total heat load), distinct from solved problems.
insights.quote: the the next big thing and kind of the last big thing is long duration high-speed flight. Um and that's the single most challenging bit of the the whole thing.

insights.claim: Hermeus runs its Quarterhorse program on a 'one miracle per year' cadence, building roughly one new airplane annually to de-risk the next step toward Darkhorse, invoking Kelly Johnson's Skunk Works adage.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: An annual de-risking cadence is the core execution thesis; missing the yearly milestone would be the clearest leading indicator of slippage.
insights.quote: famous adage from Kelly Johnson, you know, famous founder of Skunk Works and greatest aircraft designer ever to live, one miracle per program.

insights.claim: Quarterhorse 2.1 hit its first supersonic flight (about Mach 1.21) just 364 days after Mark 1's first ~30-second flight, with the Mark 2.2 airframe carrying the pre-cooler to fly later in 2026 and a high-temperature airframe (2.3) targeting Mach 3 next year.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Concrete cadence proof: hitting supersonic within a year of first flight validates the de-risk schedule; the Mach 3 / Chimera ramjet steps are the next checkpoints to track.
insights.quote: So that was achieved 364 days after Mark 1 flew. So less than a year.

insights.claim: Piplica argues no new US fighter or large military aircraft manufacturer has been founded since General Dynamics in the 1950s, and that this scarcity is paradoxically the opportunity (echoing Thiel/Musk on SpaceX and Tesla as last-mover advantages).
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: The decades-long absence of new entrants is reframed as moat and opportunity rather than a warning; central to the American Dynamism investment thesis.
insights.quote: there is a reason there has not been a new fighter jet manufacturer... founded in this country since the 1950s. General Dynamics was the last one.

themes.theme: Hermeus capital strategy and commercial path

insights.claim: Hermeus raised a $350M Series C led by Khosla (transcribed 'Cost Luck'), and Piplica expects to raise billions more while staying private, betting that customer-flight revenue will match or exceed capital needs.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Signals private markets can fund hypersonics to scale (cf. Anduril ~$11B, SpaceX ~$10B pre-IPO); the revenue-matching claim is the key assumption to stress-test.
insights.quote: there will be billions of dollars that we'll want to raise. Um but the revenue numbers that are coming alongside that um will be equal if not more.

insights.claim: Hermeus copies SpaceX's capital-efficiency 'hack' of getting customers to pay for R&D by flying its airplanes for them, with the DIU (Defense Innovation Unit) contract monetizing the envelope-expanding flight-test data much like SpaceX's original COTS contract with NASA.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Customer-funded R&D is the capital-efficiency moat that lets Hermeus stay private longer; the COTS-style contract structure is the financial mechanism investors should model.
insights.quote: they effectively got their customer to pay for their R&D by paying them to fly rockets all the time... you get your customers to pay for it. It's the greatest hack in the book.

insights.claim: Piplica sees cargo as the natural first commercial application of hypersonic aircraft (no people to certify for), citing overnight-shipped legal documents, flowers and lobsters, and framing Mach 4-5 travel as crossing the Atlantic in 90 minutes to regionalize global economies.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica, co-founder and chairman of Hermeus
insights.so_what: Maps the dual-use commercial optionality beyond defense; cargo-first de-risks certification and could expand the long-term TAM if per-flight costs fall.
insights.quote: cargo is is the like natural entry point number one because you don't need to put people on the platform.

insights.claim: Piplica draws a structural-advantage contrast where defense startups deliver hypersonic progress roughly three-to-five times faster and cheaper than primes, noting the F-22 and F-35 each took 20-25 years and $30-60B to develop.
insights.speaker: AJ Piplica and Alex (host)
insights.so_what: The cost/speed gap versus legacy primes is the crux of the defense-tech disruption thesis; quantified prime-program timelines/costs anchor the comparison.
insights.quote: the F-22 and F-35, they each took 20 to 25 years to develop and like 30 and 60 billion dollars just for development.
